data.title: Tesla: Pressure Surges After Terrible Q1
data.description:
It's been a wild five months for shareholders of Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) (TSX:TSLA:CA). The electric vehicle company saw its shares more than double after the November US election. However, the stock fell back to Earth quickly as questions regarding Elon Musk and how his political affiliation were impacting the company's brand swirled. While everyone knew that the Model Y refresh would lead to a dramatic sequential drop in Q1 deliveries, things were much worse than expected, increasing the pressure on the company as it enters a very important quarter in its history.

Previous coverage of the name:

My last article on Tesla came back in January, when the company reported its fourth quarter results. The numbers were rather disappointing, as revenues came in well short of street estimates, and a hit to margins led to a bottom line miss as well. Investors were happy that Elon Musk announced that robotaxis would be launching in June, but the company also was a little more downbeat on its yearly forecast than the CEO was just a few months prior.

The Tesla stock roller coaster has been in full swing in recent months. After almost getting to $500 a share, the stock pulled back into the Q4 report. I reiterated a sell rating at that time given the poor fundamentals, and the stock has lost roughly 31% since that last article. That's underperformance of about 25 percentage points as compared to the S&P 500.

Tesla reported Q1 production and delivery figures:

The first quarter of the year can usually be a little weaker for the auto industry, and a few less calendar days doesn't help. Tesla also normally sees a small factory shutdown for the Chinese New Year, and the Model Y refresh this year was slated to impact production and deliveries in a big way. After delivering a record 495,000 vehicle in Q4 2024, here is what Tesla announced for Q1 2025, along with 10.4 GWh of energy storage deployments.

Unfortunately for investors, things were much worse than many expected. Even though analysts had been cutting their estimates for several weeks, Tesla still fell more than 40,000 vehicles shy of the IR compiled consensus. This delivery number also represented a drop of more than 50,000 units over the prior year period, which will make it a little harder to show year-over-year growth this year. The number for other models also was surprisingly low, adding to the proof that Tesla is having trouble selling the Cybertruck at its current price point.

Q2 is when the pressure really ramps up:

With the Model Y refresh now complete, Tesla needs to show it can get its growth back on the right track. Earlier in this decade, management was looking for 50% annual growth, but those plans have hit a major speed bump in recent years, with 2024 actually showing a year-over-year delivery decline despite the initial ramp of the Cybertruck. Elon Musk surprised everyone when he called for 20%-30% delivery growth for 2025 back in October, but official guidance just says an increase this year. After the massive Q1 shortfall, it seems almost impossible for Tesla to hit that 20% growth mark.

Tesla is slated to introduce more affordable models in the first half of this year. Some believe that we'll see a smaller, hatchback like vehicle, while others are calling for stripped down versions of the Model 3 and Y. These vehicles may be even more important for the growth story if the $7,500 federal EV credit in the US disappears in the coming months. More affordable vehicles would also likely help in China a bit, where Tesla is still at more of a premium price point.

One thing is clear, and that is that street analyst estimates have been coming down considerably for more than 2 years now, as the table below shows. Investors are hoping that a new wave of delivery growth can spark a turnaround in estimates. The current estimate average calls for 11.4% revenue growth this year, with adjusted EPS growth of more than 9.1%. After the Q1 shortfall, I expect we'll see more estimate cuts coming.

The other major item coming is the launch of robotaxis in June. Tesla is still a far cry away from the million robotaxis that Elon Musk called for to be on the roads back in 2020, but this is now supposed to be the official launch for this service. The company is launching a scaled down version of the grand plan in Austin, Texas, to start, with more cities and states potentially coming throughout 2025. Tesla finds itself a bit behind in this race currently to a name like Alphabet's (GOOG) (GOOGL) Waymo, which has already topped 200,000 weekly paid rides and is announcing new city launches over time.

A look at current valuation:

When you look at Tesla, the stock itself looks extremely expensive for an automotive company. The stock finished Wednesday at more than 107 times its expected adjusted earnings for this year. As a comparison, the big two traditional US automakers average numbers that are in the single digits times their respective expected earnings. Even if you factor in Tesla as a part-tech company, the valuation is still many times that of the major tech giants, that go for 20 to 30 times their respective annual earnings.

Furthermore, in the chart below, you can see how Tesla matches up on a price-to-expected sales basis for 2025 vs. other EV names - Lucid Group, Inc. (LCID), VinFast Auto Ltd. (VFS), Rivian Automotive, Inc. (RIVN), XPeng Inc. (XPEV), BYD Company Limited (OTCPK:BYDDF), NIO Inc. (NIO), ZEEKR Intelligent Technology Holding Limited (ZK), and Polestar Automotive Holding UK PLC (PSNY). All valuations are as of Wednesday's close.

The average of the eight comparison names is 1.76 times their expected revenues for this year. A number of these other companies are projected to see much higher revenue growth rates than Tesla this year. Of course, they are all working off much smaller base numbers. Additionally, Tesla is the only one of these names that delivers any meaningful amount of profitability and cash flow currently, but the premium that shares trade for is massive. As of Wednesday's close, the average price target on the Street was about $330, implying roughly 16.6% upside from the day's close. It wouldn't surprise me if we see some target cuts coming, however, after the Q1 delivery report fell well short of expectations.

Final thoughts and recommendation:

On Wednesday, Tesla announced its Q1 delivery figure, and the result was even worse than even the most bearish expectations out there. While the Model Y refresh certainly impacted the numbers, perhaps Elon Musk's politics are hurting the brand a bit more than originally thought. As Tesla gets set to launch more affordable vehicles and start robotaxi rides this quarter, the pressure has definitely now increased given the stock's well above industry average valuation.

With the Q1 numbers being really bad, I have to keep my sell rating on the stock here today. While Q2 should show some decent sequential growth, too many potential headwinds remain, like upcoming US tariffs and the possible elimination of the US EV tax credit. The latest sales numbers will continue the trend of analysts reducing their estimates, so until those numbers start to level off a bit, the stock may remain under pressure.

data.title: ETO: Outperforming The Market And Good Peer Comps
data.description:
The Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund (NYSE:ETO) is a closed-end fund that income-seeking investors may choose to purchase in an attempt to earn a very high level of income from the assets that are already in their portfolios. This is a basic reason for purchasing almost any closed-end fund, but this one has a few reasons why investors might prefer it over some of the other ones in the market. One of these is that, as the name of the fund implies, the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund invests in dividend-paying common equities that are located all over the world. This alone makes this fund somewhat better than most income-focused funds in the market, due at least in part to the fact that the majority of funds that seek to provide investors with income do so by investing in fixed-income securities.

In a few recent articles, I showed that bonds have generally failed in their primary function as a store of value over the past ten years. We can see this in the simple fact that domestic investment-grade bonds have delivered a negative real return over the past decade:

This chart shows the return of the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, which tracks the price of domestic investment-grade bonds, over the past ten years (April 1, 2015 through today). However, unlike most performance charts, this one assumes that an investor took all of the distributions and coupons paid out by the index fund and used them to purchase additional shares of the index fund on the distribution date. In addition, the chart is also adjusted for inflation using the Consumer Price Index for all Urban Consumers. These assumptions result in the index delivering a negative 15.08% return over the ten-year period. If we add the domestic junk bond index (JNK) to this chart and make the same assumptions, we get this:

Due to the effects of inflation, once again as measured by the Consumer Price Index, even junk bonds delivered a scant 0.63% real return annually. This is certainly not an appropriate return given the amount of risk that junk bonds carry, but it is still better than the loss of purchasing power that investors in investment-grade bonds received. It is also worth noting that if the Consumer Price Index actually understates inflation, as many economists and other financial analysts believe, then junk bond real returns over the period could very easily swing negative. To my mind, then, this makes bonds uninvestible for anyone who is trying to support themselves from their portfolios. This is a category of investors that includes most retirees. After all, inflation compounds and over the course of a two- or three-decade retirement, it adds up to a lot of lost purchasing power. If your portfolio is constantly delivering negative real returns, then the quality of your lifestyle will decline over time. As such, bond funds should probably be avoided.

Equities, on the other hand, have managed to deliver positive real returns. This chart covers the same time period as the two above, but this time shows the performance of the S&P 500 Index (SPY) and the MSCI World Index (URTH):

The general assumptions remain exactly the same as with bonds. I assume that investors reinvest all distributions made by the two index funds on the date of payment and downward adjust for the Consumer Price Index. As we can see, both of these indices delivered positive real returns over the period, with the S&P 500 Index doing much better.

As such, we clearly want to be invested in equities in order to ensure that our wealth maintains its purchasing power over time. The Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund invests in common equities, so this gives the fund a real advantage over those funds that focus their efforts on fixed-income securities.

The global focus of the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund could also give it an advantage over many other closed-end funds. This is because the global focus allows it to exploit opportunities for earning profits that arise outside of the United States. In a recent article, I described one of those opportunities that has already emerged. As we all know, the equity market in the United States has not been performing very well year-to-date. Year-to-date, the S&P 500 Index (SP500) is down 3.58% while the NASDAQ-100 Index (QQQ) is down a much larger 8.15%:

This has naturally caused havoc for the share price and net asset value of any fund that invests primarily in American equities. However, there are other markets in the world that have done much better this year. For example, Europe (IEUR) is up 11.87%, Switzerland (EWL) is up 11.92%, and Japan (EWJ) is up a meager 0.77%:

An American-only equity fund could not take advantage of this profit opportunity by selling American stocks and purchasing foreign ones. However, a global fund such as the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund can. That does not mean that this fund has taken advantage of that opportunity, mind you, only that it theoretically can. We will want to take a closer look at the fund’s portfolio and recent performance to see whether or not it has taken advantage of this opportunity. The fact that it has the potential to do so, though, does give it an advantage over funds that do not have such an opportunity.

In addition to simply being able to take advantage of opportunities to invest in better-performing assets abroad, the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund has access to assets with higher yields. As of the time of writing, the MSCI All-Countries World ex-US Index (ACWX) has a yield of 2.76%. That is substantially higher than any of the broad market American stock indices:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

S&P 500 Index

1.28%

NASDAQ 100 Index

0.63%

Russell 2000 Index (IWM)

1.24%

Clearly, then, foreign equities have higher yields overall than domestic equities. The Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund has the ability to invest in foreign equities with higher yields and thus obtain a higher level of income for its investors than could be achieved through investment in American equities. Any income-focused investor should appreciate this.

Recent Performance Update

As regular readers can likely remember, we previously discussed the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund early in the winter of 2025. My previous article on this fund was published to Seeking Alpha on January 15, 2025, so about two-and-a-half months have passed since that time. As we all know, a lot of things can happen in two and a half months, and that has indeed been the case in this particular period.

Perhaps the most important thing that occurred during this period that had an impact on this fund is that the euphoria following the conclusion of the American national elections wore off and uncertainty began to cast a cloud over the market. President Trump in the United States ran on a platform of using tariffs on foreign imports to achieve his goal of reducing the American trade deficit and increasing the production of goods in the United States. Mr. Trump moved very fast on this agenda, as the financial news headlines have been dominated by statements that he has made regarding the tariffs. There have also been headlines about various foreign countries threatening their own tariffs or other trade barriers against the United States in retaliation for any tariffs that the United States imposes. It is uncertain how this whole situation will end up unfolding, and it is also uncertain what impact tariffs will even have on corporate profits. The market doesn’t like uncertainty, and as a result, this whole situation has increased market volatility. It has also been at least partially responsible for the stock market decline since the start of this year.

Most of the other factors that would have an impact on the recent performance of the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund have already been discussed. For example, the apparent rotation out of domestic assets and into foreign ones that has been causing foreign markets to rise in defiance of American equity weakness. This trend mostly means that the performance of this fund will largely depend on what proportion of its assets are invested in the United States compared to invested internationally. For example, the Thornburg Income Builder Opportunities Trust (TBLD) is overweight to Europe, and it has delivered a rather impressive performance so far this year. If the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund also had a significant allocation to European equities, then it also could have performed pretty well. Unfortunately, as we have seen in various previous articles, most Eaton Vance funds have a high allocation to the American mega-cap technology stocks and the American equity markets in general, so we should not count on that.

With that said, let us have a look at how this fund has performed since our last discussion. As just stated, my previous article was published on January 15, 2025, so here is a chart showing the fund’s performance since that date:

This is perhaps slightly disappointing, but it is not really out of line with what we would have expected. The share price of the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund declined by 3.14%, which was a better performance than the S&P 500 Index delivered over the period. The improved performance versus the S&P 500 Index was likely due to the fund’s foreign holdings. However, we can see that the fund did underperform the MSCI World Index. That could be a side effect of the fund’s use of leverage, since the leverage would amplify any declines in net asset value. As both the S&P 500 Index and the MSCI World Index declined over the period, it is likely that the assets held by the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund also suffered declines in aggregate.

As is always the case with closed-end funds, though, investors in the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund actually did better over the period than the above chart suggests. As I stated in my previous article on this fund:

The above chart does not accurately show the return that investors in the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund received over the period. This is because the modus operandi for a closed-end fund like this is to pay out all of its investment profits to the shareholders in the form of distributions while attempting to maintain a relatively stable net asset value. As such, the distributions play a much larger role in the total return that investors receive than it does in an index fund. As such, we need to include the distributions in the performance chart above to see how investors in the fund actually did.

When we take the distributions that were paid out by the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund as well as the indices over the period into account, we get this alternative to the chart above:

This certainly might improve the overall impression that potential investors get of this fund. As we can see, investors in the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund only experienced a 1.80% total loss over the period. This was actually better than investors in either of the indices would have received, which is mostly due to the fund having a substantially larger yield. In short, the distributions that were paid by the fund over the period were sufficient to offset some of the share price declines and give investors a higher total return overall. However, a smaller loss is still a loss, so the recent performance is still not as good as we might want to see.

Peer Distribution Comparison

The Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund has a substantially higher yield than any of the major market indices. As we saw earlier in this article, nearly all of the broad-market American indices have yields of under 2%. The broad market global indices that actually include the United States are not much better:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

MSCI World Index

1.49%

MSCI All-Countries World Index (ACWI)

1.83%

If we exclude the United States, equities yields increase above 2%, but those indices are not really an appropriate comparison for the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund. After all, this fund invests in both American and foreign stocks. Any investor who does not want exposure to the United States should instead invest in an international fund, so anyone buying this one is doing so because they want American exposure. As such, the above global indices are more appropriate comparisons.

The Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund has an 8.42% yield at the current stock price, so it is obviously superior to comparable indices in terms of yield. However, it is not particularly competitive with its peers in this respect. We can see that here:

The Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund is the lowest-yielding out of this peer group, and it is one of the lowest-yielding global hybrid funds currently in the market. This is almost certainly going to reduce the fund’s appeal among those investors for whom the generation of income is of highest priority. After all, there are several funds shown in this list that have yields over 10%, but this fund is much lower at 8.42%. On a $1 million investment, an investor is sacrificing over $1,000 per month in monthly income by choosing this fund rather than a higher-yielding fund such as the PIMCO Global StocksPLUS & Income Fund or the Virtus Total Return Fund. However, over the past five years, the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund has outperformed the PIMCO Global StocksPLUS & Income Fund:

As such, the best fund to choose depends largely on your goals, but the low yield is somewhat problematic from an income perspective.

With that said, the current yield of the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund does appear to be a vote of confidence from the market. As I stated in a previous article:

The market tends to value closed-end funds based on their yields. As such, when we see a fund with a yield that is substantially above that of its peers, it can be a sign that the market doubts the fund’s ability to sustain its payout.

This works in reverse as well, as a fund with a low yield when compared to its peers is generally viewed as having a sustainable distribution by the market.

The second paragraph is clearly more relevant here, as the current yield of the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund is lower than that of its peers. As such, the market seems to believe that it can sustain the current payout. This is a good sign, and indeed, in the previous article, I showed that the fund did manage to cover its distributions over the most recent fiscal year. That could make the fund somewhat more attractive.

About The Fund

According to the fund’s website, the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a very high level of total return. This objective makes a great deal of sense considering the strategy that the fund employs to achieve it, and this strategy is described in the fund’s most recent annual report:

Under normal market conditions, the Fund invests at least 80% of its total managed assets in dividend-paying common and preferred stocks of U.S. and foreign issuers that the portfolio managers believe at the time of investment are eligible to pay dividends that may constitute qualified dividend income and, therefore, qualify for federal income taxation at rates applicable to long-term capital gains. The remainder of the Fund’s portfolio may be invested in stocks and other investments that pay dividends, distributions, or other amounts taxable for federal income tax purposes at rates applicable to ordinary income.

Under normal market conditions, the Fund invests (i) at least 25% of its managed assets in securities of U.S. issuers; (ii) at least 40% of its managed assets in securities of non-U.S. issuers, including issuers located in emerging market countries, unless conditions are not deemed favorable, in which case the Fund would invest at least 30% of its total managed assets in securities of non-U.S. issuers; and (iii) in issuers located in at least five different countries (including the U.S.). The Fund may also invest up to 30% of its total assets in securities rated below investment grade.

The final sentence of this description is the reason why the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund is classified as a hybrid fund as opposed to a global equity fund. As we can see, the fund explicitly states that up to 30% of its assets will be invested in junk bonds. This is something that many potential investors in this fund may overlook, as junk bonds have a different investment performance and profile than common equities. As we saw earlier in this article, the S&P 500 Index is down fairly substantially since the start of this year. However, that is not the case with junk bonds, as the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index is only down 0.46% since the start of the year:

While junk bonds are not nearly as safe as investment-grade bonds, or so most investors usually believe, they are safer than common equities because they are still debt securities. As such, an investor will always get the face value of the bond at maturity, unless the issuing company defaults. In the case of equities, it is very possible for the company’s equity to go to zero (in the event of a liquidation). In a liquidation, bondholders, including junk bondholders, at least have a chance to get something.

As shown, junk bonds have delivered a much better performance than equities year-to-date, which could be due to investors wanting to reduce their risk as a result of the uncertainty surrounding global trade dynamics right now. We briefly discussed these dynamics earlier in this article. In short, though, the uncertainty has prompted a flight to safety trade in which investors try to get out of common equities and buy assets considered to be safer, which include junk bonds. It has not, however, been the case that junk bonds have outperformed equities over an extended period of time. Over the past ten years, for example, the S&P 500 Index has risen by 166.97%, but junk bonds are actually down 19.61%:

We can expect that, over most extended periods of time, junk bonds will underperform common stocks. As such, the presence of the junk bonds in the fund’s portfolio will reduce its performance compared to an all-equity portfolio over the long term (although it could have some beneficial effects in the short term, as we have seen year-to-date). This might be one reason why this fund has not outperformed the S&P 500 Index over the long term. Over the past ten years, shares of the fund are actually down 2.33%, but it has delivered a 127.62% total return due to its distribution:

This is significantly worse than what the S&P 500 Index delivered over the same period of time. Thus, investors here are sacrificing total return in exchange for a higher level of income, which might be acceptable for some. After all, some readers have left comments on my previous articles suggesting that they value yield and income above maximizing potential returns.

The higher the proportion of junk bonds in the portfolio, the lower the long-term returns for the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund. The last time that we discussed this fund, it only had 18.4% of its portfolio invested in junk bonds, so it was well below the 30% maximum allowable level. The fund has released an updated schedule of investments since the previous article was published, and this document tells us the fund’s asset allocation as of January 31, 2025. Here is what it provides:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets

The “Miscellaneous” category is completely new, as the fund did not have anything in its portfolio with that label on October 31, 2024 (the date on the most recent schedule of investments the last time that we looked at this fund). The securities that were added to the fund’s portfolio under this category are escrow certificates with a principal amount of $449,273:

It appears that this is simply a certificate that certain conditions were satisfied, and money was placed in escrow to be paid out to the fund at a later date. The holding is footnoted 6, 8, and 9, which simply state that this security is not registered under the Securities Act of 1933, and that the issuer is in default. Thus, what probably happened is that the issuer defaulted and under the terms of the settlement agreement with its creditors, it placed some money in escrow for them. The fund does not provide any further information about this particular security, though, so any more than that is somewhat uncertain.

Another change that we see in the fund’s asset allocation since our last discussion is that the fund’s common equity allocation declined and its allocation to money market funds increased over the period. Here is a full summary:

This change suggests that the fund may have sold some common stock and simply kept the money in cash. This makes a certain amount of sense given the current market conditions, as holding common stock (at least domestic equities) has been a losing proposition year-to-date. However, it is unclear when exactly this trade took place, what it sold, or why it made the change. As such, we cannot be certain whether this was a good move or not.

Leverage

As is the case with most closed-end funds, the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund employs leverage as a method of boosting the effective yield and total return that it earns from the assets in its portfolio. I explained how this works in my previous article on this fund:

In short, the fund borrows money and then uses that borrowed money to purchase common stocks or other assets. As long as the purchased assets deliver a higher total return than the interest rate that the fund has to pay on the borrowed money, the strategy works pretty well to boost the effective total return of the portfolio. As this fund is capable of borrowing money at institutional rates, which are considerably lower than retail rates, this will usually be the case.

However, the use of debt in this fashion is a double-edged sword. This is because leverage boosts both gains and losses. As such, we want to ensure that the fund is not using too much leverage because that would expose us to too much risk. I do not generally like to see a fund’s leverage exceed a third as a percentage of its assets for that reason.

According to CEF Data, the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund has leveraged assets comprising 18.70% of its portfolio. This is a slight increase over the 17.90% leverage that the fund had the last time that we discussed it, which is not especially surprising. After all, as we saw earlier in this article, the fund’s share price fell by 3.14% over the period. If this correlates with a fall in the fund’s net asset value, then we would expect the leverage to have increased simply because the amount that it has borrowed will now be a higher proportion of the overall portfolio, all else being equal.

It is indeed the case that the fund’s portfolio declined a bit over the period. This chart shows the fund’s net asset value from January 15, 2025 (the date of our previous discussion) through today:

As we can see, the fund’s net asset value declined by 2.51% over the period in question. This is actually a better performance than the fund’s share price delivered, so this suggests that its valuation has improved. We will get to that in a bit, but for now, we have confirmation that a decrease in the fund’s net asset value caused its leverage to increase. This is exactly what we expect.

In the previous article, we saw that the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund had a somewhat lower level of leverage than many of its peers. Here is how this fund compares today:

As we can see, the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund is one of the lesser-leveraged funds, although it is not the lowest one on the list. This overall tells us that the fund is not using an unsafe level of leverage given its strategy. As was the case before, we should not need to worry about it too much.

Valuation

Shares of the Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Opportunities Fund are currently trading at a 7.47% discount on net asset value. This is not as good a price as the 9.31% discount that the fund’s shares have had on average over the past month.

However, the fund’s current discount is fairly attractive compared to its historical valuation. Over the past three years, shares of this fund have traded at prices ranging from an 11.39% discount to a 15.57% premium on net asset value. The average price over the period was a 6.78% discount, which is a smaller discount (more expensive valuation) than what the fund has right now.

Here is how this fund currently compares to its peers in terms of valuation:

The fund therefore does not look exceedingly expensive, especially since it is currently sporting a discount that is a bit larger than its three-year average. Overall, the current price seems okay.

Expenses

data.title: How To Make Sense Of Trump's 'Tough Love' Tariffs
data.description:
U.S. stocks underperformed in the first quarter of 2025, hit by a double whammy from intensifying policy uncertainty and a U-turn in select mega-cap stocks. Into this volatile backdrop, April 2 — President Trump’s so-called Liberation Day — was circled on investors' calendars as a catalyst.

Tariff talk

Today’s announcement from the Rose Garden marked another bold policy step, charging large reciprocal tariffs against many of the United States' largest trading partners. For example, the policy includes:

  • A new 10% base tariff on most trading partners, effective April 5
  • A ramp-up a few days later to match half of the tariff and non-tariff barriers that countries impose on the U.S.

We estimate the average effective tariff rate could increase by almost 14% — a historically large increase in trade duties.

Two higher level observations are important here. First, the reciprocal tariffs leave room for the duties to get negotiated away. Case-in-point: Some very large exporters like Vietnam have already said that they will cut all tariffs on U.S. products. Second, the exemptions separate the reciprocal policy from Trump’s sector-level tariffs like steel, aluminum, and automobiles. This suggests to us that those sector-level tariffs are likely to be more permanent.

Growth hit

The administration had already pushed harder than expected on trade policy with tariffs on imports from China, Canada, Mexico, steel, aluminum, and automobiles & parts. While the policies could support domestic manufacturing over the longer term, we estimated these measures were likely to act as a drag on real GDP growth of 0.5-0.75% and a boost to core PCE inflation of 0.75%.

Today’s reciprocal tariffs could dent real GDP growth by another 0.75% and lift core PCE inflation by an additional 1%. That would likely leave the U.S. growth outlook at 1% or less for 2025 and push core inflation north of 3.5%. Accordingly, we’ve raised our estimate of U.S. recession risk in the year ahead from 30% to 35-40%.

Stunted growth?

Estimated tariff impacts on GDP for 2025 vary

Pessimism not panic

U.S. markets were moderately risk-on through Tuesday’s session. But the move reversed following Trump's announcement, with S&P 500 futures trading down over 3% from the close. 10-year Treasury yields also declined by .05%.

We remain focused on holding diversified portfolio strategies into this period of extreme policy uncertainty. And we will be closely monitoring our measures of market psychology for potential dislocations in markets. Currently, our signals show pessimism in the U.S. stock market but haven’t reached an extreme level of panic. Treasury yields at 4.1% are in-line with our estimates of fair value for bonds.

Disclosures

These views are subject to change at any time based upon market or other conditions and are current as of the date at the top of the page. The information, analysis, and opinions expressed herein are for general information only and are not intended to provide specific advice or recommendations for any individual or entity.

This material is not an offer, solicitation or recommendation to purchase any security.

Forecasting represents predictions of market prices and/or volume patterns utilizing varying analytical data. It is not representative of a projection of the stock market, or of any specific investment.

Nothing contained in this material is intended to constitute legal, tax, securities or investment advice, nor an opinion regarding the appropriateness of any investment. The general information contained in this publication should not be acted upon without obtaining specific legal, tax and investment advice from a licensed professional.

Please remember that all investments carry some level of risk, including the potential loss of principal invested. They do not typically grow at an even rate of return and may experience negative growth. As with any type of portfolio structuring, attempting to reduce risk and increase return could, at certain times, unintentionally reduce returns.

data.title: IonQ Is Trading At Dangerous, Speculation Level Valuations
data.description:
Performance Assessment

It has been a good decision to avoid being bullish on IonQ (NYSE:IONQ), but I have missed out on lot of value by not rating it a 'Sell' given the large underperformance vs the S&P500 (SPY) (SPX) (IVV) (VOO):

Thesis

I continue to believe IonQ is overhyped, even though it looks like it has the healthiest fundamentals among quantum stocks:

  1. Topline growth guidance is unimpressive vs expectations
  2. FCF burn is getting worse but the company's liquidity runway has increased
  3. ID Quantique seems like a reasonable acquisition
  4. Valuations are at a discount vs peers but quite expensive on an absolute basis
  5. Technicals look weak after a rejection from major monthly resistance
  6. Short squeeze risk is a key monitorable

If you are unfamiliar with IonQ, I encourage you to read my initiating coverage of the company.

My thesis is applicable for the warrant class as well (NYSE:IONQ.WS) exercisable at $11.50 per share since warrants convert into equity.

Topline growth guidance is unimpressive vs expectations

IonQ is growing revenues at a good YoY doubling clip, albeit from a rather low base:

And in Q4 FY24, it continued its track record of surprising on revenue delivery vs consensus estimates with a 13.9% beat:

But I care more about the guidance metric, which is more forward-looking. Here, management said we can expect $85 million +/- $10 million for the Q1 FY25 Mar'25 quarter. This was a big 54% miss as consensus had pegged in more than double that at $16.3 million:

So I think there are some concerns about revenue execution, which is especially dangerous for highly priced stocks (discussed more in the valuations section). Now, in addition to this, I also notice that going ahead, revenue pipeline visibility may be reduced as management intends to cease disclosures of the bookings metric:

Because of the early stage of the company's sales organization and the time it takes to turn a sale into revenue, we created the bookings metric back in 2021 to allow investors to gauge our progress. With revenue expected to be in the 9 figures next year 2026, we are sunsetting bookings guidance.

- CFO Thomas Kramer in the Q4 FY24 earnings call

I think it is worth noting that looking at the broader trend between bookings and revenues, I struggle to see a clear coincidence or leading relationship:

Still, I believe a combination of revenue guidance misses and cessation of useful top-line related metrics is not a great sign for GARP-oriented investors like me.

FCF burn is getting worse, but the company's liquidity runway has increased

As with any unprofitable company, a critical part of my analysis is to closely track cash burn and liquidity or funding runway. IonQ's Dec'24 quarter saw FCF cash burn worsen further to $43 million, primarily due to an 82% sequential increase in SG&A expenses from $22 million in Q3 FY24 to $39 million in Q4 FY24.

In addition to R&D, management expects to continue spending heavily on sales initiatives:

This increase was due to us growing our go-to-market function as we continue the investment in our commercialization efforts, and we expect that trend to continue as we further expand our sales initiatives.

- CFO Thomas Kramer in the Q4 FY24 earnings call

The good news is the company has bolstered their funding resources with a recent equity raise of $360 million after fees, bringing total pro forma liquidity to $700 million:

Taking the TTM average quarterly FCF burn of $31 million, I compute that there are about 22-23 quarters or 5.7 years of funding runway left. I think this is quite solid and so there are no major risks here in the short to medium term. In any case, the ability of the company to raise money when required is an advantage that I believe is augmented by the new CEO - Niccolo de Masi since:

In total, he [Niccolo de Masi] has raised more than $3 billion in equity to support public and private companies that he has led.

- IONQ Press Release

ID Quantique seems like a reasonable acquisition

Recently, IonQ announced that it was buying quantum network encryption company ID Quantique to bolster its portfolio of quantum networking patents:

Once the deal closes, IonQ will consolidate ID Quantique's global book of business, more than 100 team members and gain control of almost 300 quantum networking patents and patent applications. In aggregate, IonQ will shortly control nearly 900 patents and patent applications across quantum key distribution, quantum sensing, quantum networking and quantum computing… With ID Quantique, IonQ furthers its leadership in quantum networking. Our goal is to build a suite of products that enable secure quantum communication in all forms, from satellites to ground stations across existing telecommunication fiber infrastructure to drones on a battlefield. Those following IonQ closely will note that we are building out the key component technologies for the telecom and defense sectors.

- Executive Chairman Peter Chapman in the Q4 FY24 earnings call

By my math, the $156 million consideration paid corresponded to an EV/Revenue multiple of 6.94x, assuming that ID Quantique has annual revenues of $22.5 million:

I think this is quite a reasonable price given that quantum companies are trading at much, much higher valuations currently:

Valuations are at a discount vs peers but quite expensive on an absolute basis

Considering a set of IonQ and 2 other major quantum computing stocks - Rigetti Computing (RGTI) and D-Wave Quantum (QBTS), IONQ's 58x 1-yr fwd EV/Revenue multiple corresponds to a decent 42% discount vs the group's average multiple of almost 100x:

Note that even after adjusting the EV calculation to reflect the additional $360 million raise in Q1 FY25, the implied 1-yr fwd EV/Revenue multiple for IONQ still comes in at 57x, assuming a FY25 revenue estimate of $85.42 million.

But although IONQ is trading at a discount to other quantum computing stocks, I believe a 57x revenue multiple is still too high for comfort on an absolute basis, especially given the revenue guidance misses and flattish revenue expectations going ahead (grey line in the chart below):

Technicals look weak after a rejection from major monthly resistance

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of IONQ vs SPX500

Relative to the S&P500, IonQ has rejected from a major monthly resistance level, and it looks like there is still some space to go until it reaches a minor monthly support area. Hence, I anticipate further downside to follow, implying underperformance vs the S&P500 going ahead.

Short squeeze risk is a key monitorable

IonQ has been a rather popular short as its short interest levels have consistently been above 10% over the past 2 years:

Currently, the short interest is at 11.68%. I think levels of 15% or 20% and higher may increase the chances of crowded bearish views and hence upside risk in IONQ stock via a short squeeze. So how the short interest evolves over time is something on my radar.

Takeaway & Positioning

IonQ is doubling revenues YoY, but that is from a low base and its guidance is well below expectations. The company's management is also ceasing disclosures of bookings numbers this year, which further reduces my confidence on top-line visibility.

On the P&L side, the company's FCF cash burn is increasing due to increased investments in sales go-to-market and some R&D. These investments are expected to continue going ahead. But the bright side is that the company has adequate funding for more than 5 years at current cash burn levels. Also, it has a good track record of raising money when required, which, I believe, is only aided by the appointment of Niccolo de Masi as the new CEO as he has a background in raising money for growth companies.

In a bid to expand its intellectual property presence in quantum networking technologies, IonQ recently announced an acquisition of ID Quantique. I compute that the price paid for this business would be rather reasonable given the high trading multiples of quantum computing stocks today.

On that note, IonQ is trading at a nice 42% discount to quantum computing stocks' averages on a 1-yr fwd EV/Revenues basis. But on an absolute basis, I think the 57x multiple is still too high for comfort. The technical charts vs the S&P500 also suggest downside risk over the next few months.

So overall, I rate the stock a 'Sell'. A key source of upside risk would be a short squeeze, as IonQ seems like it has been a popular short. To gauge this risk, I am keeping a close eye on the short interest trends.

I also rate the IONQ warrants (IONQ.WS) a 'Sell', but on a relative basis, I would rate the warrants more favorably than IONQ stock as the former has a lower exercise price of $11.50.

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Neutral/hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis

Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.

data.title: Novo Nordisk: Not As Bullish As Many
data.description:
Introduction

Novo Nordisk (NVO) shares have plummeted in the recent months, falling ~50% from the DKK 1,028 highs in June 2024.

This big drop has been fairly steady aside from a single day 18% drop in December post poor trial results of the CagriSema drug trial where results didn't meet Wall Street or management estimates. The stock hasn't really recovered since then as this has been combined with a series of other negative catalysts which have all pressured the stock price.

In this article, I'll touch on the positives and opportunities ahead, but primarily focus on why I don't think this is a buying opportunity like many analysts here on Seeking Alpha do.

Investment Case

NVO has built up a very strong market share in the diabetes and weight loss category at 54.8%, up from 50.4% in 2020 showing continued dominance and little sign of this market share collapsing.

Also, the premise of this article is a more bearish stance. Here are the positives I see for NVO at the moment (not considering valuation just yet):

  • Amycretin: Results on January 25 showed that this weight loss drug was on track to have some very positive results as numbers showed slightly less than CagriSema, but this was achieved over a much shorter time frame. This drug is in the very early stages, so it's difficult to make a full bull case out of this, but early signs appear good.
  • Market share hasn't changed just yet: Despite the bear cases calling for competition heating up, looking at the data currently suggests that NVO has not lost market share in the diabetes or GLP-1 markets. In the insulin market, they have slightly declined, but the numbers are not enough to suggest that their advantage here is in danger.
  • Operationally, NVO is very good with a solid net income margin that has remained very stable over time despite the challenges that they've faced. It's evidence that the diabetes and obesity market is a very profitable business segment.
  • Patent expirations are a long time away: Ozempic's patent is not set to expire in the US until 2032, so there's quite clearly 7+ years of growth in the market ahead (not including drugs such as CagriSema and Amycretin).
  • Financial position: Relative to competitors like Eli Lilly (LLY), NVO has a much better balance sheet with a debt to equity ratio of ~50% compared to LLY which is +200%.

Bear Case

Despite the above, I think it's better to be cautious with NVO today.

Competition

Firstly, LLY is much more diversified than NVO with a focus in oncology, neurology, endocrine, immunology, on top of diabetes and weight loss. This massively reduces regulatory risks, which is one of the key issues facing NVO today, as LLY can easily lean into different products if one is under more scrutiny. NVO does not have this luxury.

On top of this, NVO's pipeline is much less than LLY who has 77 products relative to NVO's 47 products. From purely a growth perspective, you'd probably prefer to invest in a company like LLY.

However, the real issue to NVO comes from the fact that LLY are actively challenging them in their main markets (obesity and diabetes) where LLY's diabetes treatment Mounjaro grew at 60% whilst NVO grew revenues at 30%. I'd feel a lot more confident in NVO if they were completely dominating the obesity market, but competition is starting to creep in as peers realize the high potential for growth in this market, and therefore NVO's less diversified strategy could be a big risk.

There's also risks from smaller companies like Hims & Hers (HIMS) who are actively offering compounded version of GLP-1 drugs like Ozempic and Wegovy. However, I think this is much less of a risk because HIMS are much smaller, and secondly there's far more regulatory issues with compounded drugs.

Political Risks

As I touched on above, having a very undiversified product pipeline can lead to a lot of political and regulatory risks, which is another bear argument for NVO today.

Firstly, the FDA has been focused on the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) which allows Medicare to negotiate drug prices, and hence lead to much lower revenues for drugs from NVO. GLP-1 is a very high-cost drug, and therefore this is central to these pricing regulations.

Secondly, NVO's GLP-1 drugs like Ozempic have been subject to lots of concerns regarding side effects such as cardiovascular risks, pancreatitis, and gastrointestinal issues. On top of this, you've also got patent expirations, and supply chain issues.

The main thing I want to note here is that most companies, including LLY are facing these kinds of risks too, but these are magnified at NVO because a huge portion of their revenue comes directly from GLP-1 and other weight loss drugs. These risks weigh heavier on the overall business compared to LLY where weight loss makes up a much smaller portion of the portfolio.

Valuation

There are a couple of different ways to look at the valuation, which I'll touch on here. Firstly, from a historical basis:

From a Fwd PE multiple, NVO trades far below historical averages at just 17.2x, whilst over the last 3 years the PE multiple has not regularly dropped below 25x.

Even from a 10Y chart, we are now approaching the base of 2020. In that time:

  • Revenue has increased 129%
  • EBIT has increased 159%
  • Net margins have increased 260 bps.

Considering fundamentally we haven't seen too much of a change in the actual numbers, this could be a great time to invest in NVO based on historical data.

However, the issue with historical data is that it's not considering whether the previous market was undervalued or overvalued, and I think the GLP-1 hype over the last 3 years or so perhaps inflated NVO's valuation too much. Therefore, I prefer to be a bit more forward-looking and look at the forecasts ahead, which I've done here:

Despite the diabetes and weight loss market being huge, and growing at 8.6% CAGR through to 2029 valuing the market at $72.2 billion. Therefore, if NVO doesn't gain any more market share, this provides them with a $38.9 billion opportunity in diabetes (assuming 54% share) which at 4x Sales puts them at $156 billion, which is just over 50% of the current market cap today. Given that 71% of NVO's revenue comes from diabetes today, this isn't a promising sign and to me signals that NVO still hasn't reached a point where they are undervalued enough to invest into.

Conclusion

I don't think NVO should be labelled a "Sell" position, nor do I think it's a "Buy" so I initiated a "Hold" position even though it's not part of my portfolio. Some parts of the valuation make it seem attractive, whilst other angles make it less so. Overall, I think there's a bit too many risks to make it a screaming buy for me.

Of course, if you look at it from purely a historical basis, then NVO is undervalued, but that doesn't consider a lot of factors.

k

data.title: Labrador Iron Ore Royalty - All About The Iron Ore Price
data.description:
Whither iron ore?

The iron ore price, more than anything else right now, depends upon what happens in China's property market. This is just one of those things.

The reasoning is simple enough. In the mature economies, the US, most of Europe etc, when we decide to build something new we're tearing down something old. This is even true of cars - the total stock of cars on the roads doesn't change very much, for a new car arriving upon them is usually balanced by an old car coming off them. I went through this with Radius Recycling here. This means there's a constant supply of scrap steel from which the new steel can be made. Well over 50% of US steel use comes from scrap for example.

When we've a nation developing for the first time - as China is - then this isn't going to be true. As there's not something steel containing being torn down, there's not the supply of scrap. Therefore, those new buildings - that new nation - are built with "virgin" steel. Which means that it's made from iron ore, not scrap.

A billion and more people building their first advanced civilisation - China - is therefore the major consumer of iron ore globally. What happens there, and especially in the property sector, is what drives that iron ore price, globally.

Sure, there are still different price drivers, different grades and ores gain different prices, different forms (fines, pellets etc) and so on. But the underlying price that all these different grades and markets work off is that China price.

So, China matters to the global iron ore price

An iron ore miner

Labrador Iron Ore Royalty (OTCPK:LIFZF) is not, in fact, an iron ore miner, nor does it even sell it. As I once described it:

The income of the company comes in two parts. One is a straight royalty on the sales price of the iron ore that comes up out of the mine. This is not adjusted for production costs nor anything else. It's a straight 7% off the top.

...

There's also, though a second source of income here. Labrador has a 15% or so share in the underlying mine that does the production. That's operated by Rio Tinto and their accounts for this mine are here. There's more here. Labrador has no exposure to the costs or debts of that company. It does though receive a profits distribution commensurate with its shareholding, over and above that 7% royalty off the top.

What this really is, is that to gain access to the mine, it was necessary to agree to give the government of Labrador some royalties. This is as it should be, the mere existence value of a mineral should belong to the country. Labrador then bundled those rights up and sold them as a company. They got the capital payment upfront, the investors have been getting the royalties ever since.

Worth noting that since that description the stock price is down 25% or so, but the total return is up 19%.

For the policy of the company is that those royalties, and that profit share, should flow through directly to shareholders. They claim, over time, to keep only 1% to handle the administration expenses.

The mine

The mine itself is run by Rio Tinto and isn't about to run out anytime soon:

The iron ore deposits in the Labrador City area occur as specular hematite and magnetite, generally in the ratio of 65:35. IOC has mineral reserves and resources of 1.1 billion tonnes and 1.7 billion tonnes, respectively, with an average grade of approximately 38% iron.

....

production capacity is approximately 23.3 million tonnes per year

Note what "reserves" means in this context. What has been proven to be there, is mineable. They're not about to run out.

Accounts

The Royalty company accounts are here. But they're really pretty irrelevant. Other than its own admin expenses it's a pass through operation. We can get operations and production numbers from Rio Tinto. If we ever get to worrying about Rio Tinto's ability to remain a going concern then we've all got larger problems than iron ore royalties.

Iron ore price

What this all means is that the determinant of future income from LIFZF is what's the iron ore price? The royalties are a straight 7% of product shipped plus a 15% share of mine profits. Iron ore go up, dividends go up. Or, of course, down.

IOC sells CFS based on the Platts index for 65% Fe, CFR China (the "65% Fe index"). All references to tonnes and per tonne prices in this report refer to wet metric tonnes, other than references to Platts quoted pricing, which refer to dry metric tonnes. Historically, IOC's wet ore contains approximately 3% less ore per equivalent volume than dry ore. In 2024, the average price for the 65% Fe index was US$123 per tonne, a decrease of 6% year over year. In addition to the reduction in iron ore prices, pellet premiums were lower as steel producers, faced with continuing low profit margins, substituted high quality pellets with cheaper, lower quality iron feed. The monthly Atlantic Blast Furnace 65% Fe pellet premium index as quoted by Platts (the "pellet premium") averaged US$40 per tonne in 2024, a decrease of 10% from 2023.

There are some mild variations in quantity produced given wildfires, plant maintenance and so on. But the big effect upon the dividend is that iron ore price.

So, any estimation of whether it's worth being in the stock is a guess, a bet, an estimation, of that future iron ore price.

Note this is a slightly different price index from the pricing one, but still useful to see variance. Over longer periods the variance can be extreme - in the last 15 years briefly as low as $40 and - again briefly - over $200.

We can only estimate

We can only estimate that future iron ore price, and that's going to be driven by estimations of the Chinese and global economies. I think that the Chinese property market is finally going to find past debt-driven excesses catch up with it. I therefore expect that driving force of the iron ore price to decline. Not tomorrow, but within the next couple of years of likely investment timescale for us. So, something dependent upon the global iron ore price seems not a good idea to me.

Why am I wrong?

Clearly, if the global iron ore market moves the other way, then I will be wrong. For that will feed through directly into a higher dividend.

Given that there's really nothing else here than the royalty and dividend, that's the major way I could be wrong too.

My opinion

I tend to think that the iron ore price is more likely to go down than up. True, I've thought that for some time given China's property problems and only been partially right - it is down, but not as far as I thought it would be.

A royalty play that depends upon the iron ore price thus seems like a not good idea to me.

There is one more issue here. Back that 4 years when I first recommended LIFZF the yield was 10.5%. At a time of low interest rates worldwide. Now interest rates are back up to historically normal levels, but the yield here is 6.6%. Yes, there are special issues here. But still. I don't think it's the good value it once was.

To me, "sell" means "get out" or even "go short", and "hold" means here's my opinion now make up your own mind. So while this is strongly suggesting not buying, it is, by my ranking system, a hold still.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Japan And South Korea Pivot To Negotiating Lower U.S. Tariffs
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang

Japan and South Korea will try to negotiate to lower the tariffs

Both Japan and South Korea will actively seek to reduce President Trump’s reciprocal tariffs of 24% and 25%, respectively. The largest US exports for both countries is automobiles, which were already hit with a 25% tariff. As such, the marginal impact of these new tariffs, over all, is likely to be smaller than for autos. Meanwhile, semiconductors and pharma are exempted from tariffs. Both are major export items from Japan and Korea. Thus, negative impacts should be more concentrated in heavy industry and machineries.

Neither country is likely to retaliate. Instead, they’re likely to make concessions by importing more US products. This might include gas and oil, opening up agricultural markets, increasing military spending, making direct investments in the US, and easing non-tariff barriers to imports. For example, Korean major carmaker Hyundai Motors already announced a $21 billion investment. It includes a $7.6 billion EV plant in Georgia and a $5.8 billion steel mill in Louisiana. We expect to see more such efforts from both countries, which will ultimately provide more leverage in the negotiations. They’re also likely to emphasise their status as key strategic US allies in the region.If the US wants to make technological progress and widen the gap with China, it will definitely need the support of Japan and Korea.

Impact on Japan

Global risk-off trends work in favour of Japanese asset markets in general. The USDJPY was the strongest among the G10 currencies, trading at 147.7, while 10-year Japanese government bond (JGB) yields slid to 1.36% from the previous high of 1.55%. Yet, the Nikkei Stock Index plunged 3%.

The Bank of Japan already mentioned it will continue policy normalisation efforts despite uncertainties surrounding the tariffs. Moreover, a former BoJ board member Seiji Adachi, who recently retired, said the central bank could raise rates in May if tariffs don’t trigger global market turmoil. We still have almost a month until the next BoJ meeting. So, it’s important to see how global markets react to US tariffs.

We believe that the BoJ has recently shown more concern about upside risks to inflation. As such, the upcoming inflation report will be important to watch. We also believe that the recent rally in the JGB market provides better conditions for the BoJ to deliver a hike. For the time being, we maintain our call for a BoJ hike in May. But the possibility of a delay until June has increased. We will revisit this call depending on how financial market conditions evolve and the results of the inflation readings.

Impact on South Korea

The initial market reaction to the US tariffs was negative. The KOSPI fell over 2%, three-year Korean treasury bond (KTB) futures rose to their highest since 22 March and the USDKRW opened at 1,472.

The KRW bears watching. The weak KRW apparently added to inflationary pressures, as suggested by yesterday's consumer price index (CPI) reading. Further KRW depreciation is likely to add to inflationary pressures. At the same time, we expect weaker demand pressures amid sluggish domestic demand and slower exports in the coming months.

Moreover, the government has already promised to curb inflation by freezing utility tariffs and stabilising fresh food prices. These conflicting factors complicate the Bank of Korea's (BoK) rate decision. We now expect inflation to accelerate until the end of the second quarter and then ease again in the second half. The BoK is expected to pause its rate-cut cycle at the April meeting. But markets are speculating whether it will cut in May or extend its pause until the third quarter.

We expect the KRW to weaken in 2Q25, when sentiment will be at its worst. This is when the outlook for trade negotiations – and whether tariffs come down – still won’t be known. Korean investors are likely to take a wait-and-see approach to overseas investment around the 1,500 level. Meanwhile, lower valuations for Korean-denominated assets may be more attractive to foreign investors once initial market jitters have passed.

Our base case scenario is still for a May cut. But it will depend on tomorrow's impeachment decision by the Constitutional Court and the foreign-exchange market reaction to news on President Yoon’s fate. On the fiscal policy side, we expect further support from the government in the form of emergency measures to help industry. The government is already planning a ten-trillion-KRW supplementary budget, but it could be larger depending on the agreement between the major parties.

data.title: CDT Insider Sentiment March 2025: The 2025 Market Correction
data.description:
Market Correction. As concern over tariff paralysis and the prospect of stickier inflation continued to challenge investor confidence in a historically expensive market (note the negative Excess Earnings Yield above), in March, the S&P 500 dropped more than -5.6% and at the mid-month lows, the index officially entered correction territory.

A correction is typically defined as a decline of -10% or more from a recent high (achieved back in February). Note that the S&P 500’s companion benchmark, the technology heavy, Nasdaq Composite was down over -10.4% for the month, marking the index’s sharpest one-month decline since December 2022.

The most concerning economic data point of March, which further rattled investor sentiment, was consumer sentiment. The University of Michigan’s Consumer Sentiment Index fell to 57 in March from 64.7 in February, its lowest reading since November 2022.

Simultaneously, in the same report, future inflation expectations jumped to 4.9% over the next year, up from 4.3% the previous month – a far cry from the Federal Reserve target of 2%. With consumers concerned about inflation and corporate decision-makers dazed and confused by tariff policy, the prospect of the “R” word has increased – at least by the market’s calculations.

Preparation is half the battle. As the pilots of your financial journey with CDT, our mission is to always deliver for our partners, no matter what the conditions are outside. Through turbulence, in bad or good visibility or even through a storm, our guiding principle is to Invest Above the Clouds.

Pilots of all skill levels can look good in blue skies; it is when there are dark clouds ahead that the experience of the flight crew truly matters. While no lives are at stake in financial markets, to us, the safety of the financial wellbeing of our partners is equally important.

As a flight crew with tens of thousands of hours under our belts and the tools to navigate any skies, CDT proved once again in the first quarter of this year that we can deliver for our partners even when the conditions outside are worrying.

As a “long-only” hedge fund, we get a lot of unnecessary flack for not following the traditional hedge fund long-short model. For so many reasons, we think the L&S model is flawed, one of those ideas that sounds nice on paper, but is terribly difficult to bring into the real world, so we do something different. Being different is our strength. While many of our peers and market averages are struggling year-to-date, we continue to deliver returns, so how do we do it?

Insiders. In our annual letter, we noted that most market participants from retail investors to institutional MBA money managers were all doing the exact same thing – buying into the prospect of year number 3 of an AI-hyped market frenzy led by the anointed Magnificent 7. While at the same time, our definition of the smart-money, the insiders or the people responsible for managing those businesses were selling (article).

For us, the simple prescription was obvious, steer clear. While investor attention and capital flowed to the same names over and over again, the insiders of great, undervalued businesses were buying their unloved shares.

In that spirit, in our annual letter, we challenged investors to follow what we dubbed, the CDT 7, which by our measure are high-quality businesses, with attractive valuations in which we recently observed extraordinary insider purchase activity.

The result. While the Mag 7 has returned -15.8% year-to-date, the CDT 7 has returned over +4%- that is close to a +20% delta.

Note: The results of the CDT 7 are even better if you consider dividends in total returns.

Leveraging extraordinary insider activity to direct our investment strategy is just one half of the story. Zooming out, we can also leverage insider activity to direct our reserve strategy. As you know, we don’t short stocks or other securities to offset our market risk, instead we manage that risk with reserves comprised of cash and short-duration fixed income securities.

This strategy is easily scalable, we don’t worry that the treasury will run out of bonds to sell us (article) and when the stock market hits a brick wall of volatility, our reserve acts as a shock absorber. All the while, depending on the day and the mood of the Federal Reserve, we earn 4-5% on our patience.

Among other data points informing our quantitative risk reserve strategy, like a historically stretched market valuation, we observed a significant pullback in insider purchase activity in January into February and so, we entered March in a defensive position and heavily reserved at 27% of the value of our fund – a record high.

As we mentioned in our March monthly update, we suspected that as markets continued to work through a period of volatility, we would deploy our liquidity - and we did just that.

As the S&P 500 declined -6% at the mid-month low, overall market valuations improved, and insider activity increased significantly, allowing us to meaningfully reduce our reserve to under 18% - a near 10% pt. move from February. You can see the recent history and relationship between insider sentiment, stock market returns and our reserve balance in the charts above.

For us, Above the Clouds is a promise to our partners to put their financial safety above all else. We are experienced managers leveraging the insights derived from proprietary technology that we built in-house to detect and quickly act on extraordinary insider activity to navigate any flying conditions.

We look forward to updating you from the cockpit again in May.

CDT Insider Sentiment

Objective: Predictive model that measures the historical relationship between insider sentiment and the future probability of downside volatility (risk)

Insider Trading Activity: Purchase activity of an insider’s own stock filtered by proprietary parameters to scrub noisy data

Insight: Executive-level insider sentiment is an indicator of near-term financial market risk

- Low executive sentiment suggests a high level of risk

- High executive sentiment suggests a low level of risk

Scale: A ratio of current insider trading activity in relation to historical patterns

- (0 to ∞) with a historical median measure of 1

- Below 1 implies an above normal level of risk

- Above 1 implies a below normal level of risk

Frequency: The measure is updated daily and has historically been subject to swift and possibly extreme shifts

data.title: Reciprocal Tariffs Send Oil Lower
data.description:
By Warren Patterson and Ewa Manthey

Energy - Tariffs weigh on oil

Oil prices are under pressure this morning, following other risk assets lower, after the Trump administration unveiled a base tariff of 10% on all imports from all trading partners. WTI was down more than 3% at one stage in the early morning session and trading below US$70/bbl.

President Trump’s latest levies will come into effect on April 5. An additional tariff will be implemented on some trading partners, effective April 9. For example, the reciprocal tariff on China is 34%, while the EU faces 20%. Meanwhile, Canada and Mexico are exempt from these latest tariffs. Oil and natural gas also appear to be exempt.

The scale of some of Trump’s tariffs will raise global demand concerns. There’s also increased uncertainty, with markets waiting to see how trading partners retaliate.

Away from tariffs, OPEC+ is holding a call today to discuss the need for members to stick to production targets. OPEC+ is set to bring an additional 138k b/d of supply back to the market this month, part of plans to start gradually unwinding 2.2m b/d of supply cuts. We may also get clarity on whether the group will continue to unwind supply cuts next month. Yet, even as OPEC+ brings supply back to the market, some members need to make compensation cuts for previous overproduction. This should more than offset the planned supply increases.

The Energy Information Administration’s (EIA) weekly inventory report was fairly bearish, with US crude oil inventories increasing by 6.17m barrels over the last week. Cushing crude oil inventories increased by 2.37m barrels. The large build was driven by a 728k b/d week-on-week drop in crude exports. Crude oil imports increased by 271k b/d WoW. Furthermore, refinery utilisation fell by 1pp to 86%. For refined products, gasoline stocks fell by 1.55m barrels, while distillate stocks increased by 264k barrels.

Metals - Tariff relief

Gold hit a record high following the announcement of reciprocal tariffs, with spot prices nearing US$3,170/oz at one stage. These tariffs will raise global growth concerns. There will also be uncertainty over how trading partners retaliate, which is likely to continue to support gold.

However, the tariff announcement did provide some relief to metal markets. It's becoming clear that steel and aluminium imports won't be subject to these tariffs. The same with gold and copper. The exemption for gold means that we could start seeing a slowing of gold flows into the US.
